thesis: Rocket Lab is the most credible publicly traded challenger to SpaceX in the commercial launch market. The company is evolving from a small-launch provider into a vertically integrated end-to-end space platform through acquisitions (Motiv Space Systems, Mynaric AG) and the pending $8B acquisition of Iridium Communications. The Neutron medium-lift vehicle remains the key technical inflection point, with a successful full-duration AVac engine test completed in July 2026 and a first flight still targeted for late 2026. The Iridium deal adds a $872M-revenue, 57%-EBITDA-margin satellite services business, global L-band spectrum, and 2.5M subscribers, but it also brings $3.6B in bridge financing, integration risk, and likely equity dilution. At $76.20, the stock is down ~29% from the June update and ~50% from its May 2026 all-time high, improving the risk/reward for speculative investors who can tolerate execution and integration risk through mid-2027.

drivers: Neutron first flight successful in late 2026 and commercial bookings accelerate
Iridium acquisition closes mid-2027 and delivers expected synergies ($872M revenue base, 57% EBITDA margin)
SDA/DoD awards multiple Tranches to Rocket Lab, adding $200M+ in high-margin revenue
Space Systems margins expand to 50%+ as satellite bus production scales
Company achieves EBITDA breakeven by late 2026/early 2027 and positive FCF by 2027

base_case.assumptions: Neutron first flight occurs in late 2026 with minor delays; commercial ramp begins H1 2027. Iridium deal closes with manageable integration costs and modest equity dilution. Steady government contract wins sustain $250M+ annual launch revenue. Space Systems grows 40%+ YoY with modest margin expansion. Company reaches EBITDA breakeven by mid-2027. Stock trades at 8–10x forward sales, reflecting solid execution but no multiple expansion.

risks: Neutron first flight delayed into 2027 or experiences anomaly
Iridium acquisition faces regulatory hurdles, integration costs exceed plan, or equity dilution is larger than expected
$3.6B bridge loan and additional debt/equity financing strain the balance sheet
Government contract awards disappoint; competition from SpaceX and emerging launch providers intensifies
Space Systems growth decelerates due to customer concentration or supply chain issues
Macro risk-off environment compresses growth stock multiples

catalysts.event: Neutron first flight — binary event validating medium-lift strategy; AVac full-duration test completed July 2026
Expected close of $8B Iridium acquisition — transformative for revenue scale and spectrum assets
Q2 2026 earnings (August 2026) — backlog conversion and margin progress post-Iridium announcement
SDA/DoD contract awards — high-margin government revenue expansion
Multi-launch contract execution — 5 Neutron + 3 Electron launches for confidential customer
Electron cadence increase and Archimedes engine test campaign — higher launch frequency improves unit economics

key_risks: Neutron development risk — medium-lift rocket development is technically complex and capital intensive; delays or anomaly would hurt sentiment
Iridium acquisition execution — $8B deal, $3.6B bridge loan, integration complexity, and potential equity dilution
Government dependency — significant revenue from DoD/SDA; budget cuts or contract delays would hurt
Competition — SpaceX dominates launch; other startups (Relativity, ABL) emerging; Starlink is a powerful satellite services rival
Balance sheet / financing risk — acquisition funding may require additional debt or equity, diluting shareholders
Execution risk — scaling manufacturing for launch, space systems, and Iridium integration simultaneously is operationally complex
Macro / valuation risk — high-growth stock vulnerable to rate shocks and risk-off sentiment; stock is down ~50% from May 2026 highs
